questions.question: What are naturally occurring magnets called?
questions.answer: Lodestones.
questions.solution: Naturally occurring magnets were discovered in ancient times and are known as lodestones.

questions.question: Name three metals that are attracted to a magnet.
questions.answer: Iron, Nickel, and Cobalt.
questions.solution: Materials like iron, nickel, and cobalt are magnetic and are attracted towards a magnet.

questions.question: What are the two poles of a magnet?
questions.answer: North pole and South pole.
questions.solution: Every magnet has two ends called the North pole and the South pole.

questions.question: True or False: A freely suspended magnet always rests in the East-West direction.
questions.answer: False.
questions.solution: A freely suspended magnet always comes to rest in the North-South direction.

questions.question: What is the small device used for finding directions using a magnetic needle?
questions.answer: Magnetic compass.
questions.solution: A magnetic compass was developed to find directions based on the directive property of magnets.

questions.question: Which pole of a compass needle is usually painted red?
questions.answer: The North-seeking pole (North pole).
questions.solution: The end of the needle which rests in the North direction is usually painted red for identification.

questions.question: Explain the difference between magnetic and non-magnetic materials with examples.
questions.answer: Magnetic materials are attracted to magnets (e.g., iron, nickel); non-magnetic ones are not (e.g., wood, plastic).
questions.solution: Materials that stick to a magnet are called magnetic (e.g., iron, cobalt). Materials that do not stick are called non-magnetic (e.g., rubber, glass, plastic).

questions.question: Where is the magnetic strength of a bar magnet maximum?
questions.answer: At its poles (the two ends).
questions.solution: By rolling a magnet in iron filings, we observe that most filings stick near the ends, which are the poles, indicating maximum magnetic strength there.

questions.question: Can a single magnetic pole exist? Justify.
questions.answer: No, poles always exist in pairs.
questions.solution: If a magnet is broken into pieces, each piece will still have a North and a South pole. A single isolated pole cannot exist.

questions.question: Why does a freely suspended magnet always point in the North-South direction?
questions.answer: Because the Earth behaves like a giant magnet.
questions.solution: A freely suspended magnet aligns itself with the North-South direction due to the interaction with Earth's own magnetic field.

questions.question: What is 'matsya-yantra' (machchhyantra) and its historical use?
questions.answer: An ancient Indian magnetized fish device used by sailors for naviation at sea.
questions.solution: Matsya-yantra was an iron piece shaped like a fish, magnetized and kept in a vessel of oil, used by Indians for finding directions at sea.

questions.question: Describe the basic rule of attraction and repulsion between two magnets.
questions.answer: Like poles repel each other, whereas unlike poles attract each other.
questions.solution: The North-North or South-South poles of two magnets repel, while a North-South pairing attracts.

questions.question: How can you identify a piece of metal as a magnet using only another magnet?
questions.answer: Repulsion is the surest test for identifying a magnet.
questions.solution: If one end of the metal is repelled by a pole of a magnet, it is confirmed as a magnet. Attraction alone doesn't prove it, as non-magnetized iron is also attracted.

questions.question: Briefly describe the 'stroking method' to make your own magnet.
questions.answer: Repeatedly rubbing a magnet along the length of an iron piece in one direction.
questions.solution: Place an iron bar on a table. Move one pole of a magnet along its length 30-40 times, lifting it each time and starting from the same end, without changing the pole or direction.

questions.question: Explain an activity to show that magnetic forces can act through non-magnetic materials.
questions.answer: Place a cardboard/glass between a magnet and iron objects; the magnet will still attract them.
questions.solution: Using a cardboard or thin plastic sheet between a bar magnet and a compass needle shows that the needle still deflects, proving magnetic effect acts through them.

questions.question: Why are 'keepers' used for storing bar magnets and how are they arranged?
questions.answer: To prevent demagnetization over time.
questions.solution: Bar magnets are kept in pairs with unlike poles on the same side, separated by a piece of wood, with two pieces of soft iron (keepers) placed across the ends.

questions.question: State three things that can cause a magnet to lose its properties.
questions.answer: Heating, hammering, and dropping from a height.
questions.solution: Magnets can become demagnetized if they are heated, hammered, or dropped roughly.

questions.question: If you are given three identical metal bars and only two are magnets, how do you identify them using only the bars?
questions.answer: By checking for repulsion between any two or all ends of the bars.
questions.solution: Bring the ends of the bars together. The non-magnetic iron piece will be attracted by all ends of both magnets. However, the two magnets will repel each other at their like poles.

questions.question: Why should magnets be kept away from mobile phones and television sets?
questions.answer: To avoid demagnetization and interference with the devices' electronics.
questions.solution: Strong magnets can interfere with the data and functioning of electronic devices like remotes, mobile phones, and computers.

questions.question: How can you find the North pole of a bar magnet which has no markings?
questions.answer: By suspending it freely; the end pointing North is the North pole.
questions.solution: Suspend the bar magnet using a thread. Once it comes to rest, the end that seeks the geographical North direction is the North pole.

questions.question: What happens when you bring the South pole of a magnet near the North pole of a compass needle?
questions.answer: They attract each other and the needle moves closer.
questions.solution: Since they are unlike poles, they will exhibit attraction.

questions.question: Explain why sailors in olden times used the property of magnets for navigation.
questions.answer: Because magnets provide a reliable way to find directions when landmarks or stars are not visible.
questions.solution: The sailors utilized the constant North-South directive property of suspended magnets (and later compasses) to navigate through storms or cloudy skies.

questions.question: Are all parts of a bar magnet equally magnetic? Substantiate.
questions.answer: No, the poles are more magnetic than the center.
questions.solution: Iron filings observations show that maximum filings stick to the ends (poles), while very few stick to the middle, proving unequal magnetic distribution.

questions.question: What is the shape of the magnet inside a laboratory compass?
questions.answer: The shape of a needle.
questions.solution: A magnetic compass contains a thin magnetic needle mounted on a pivot point.

questions.question: Describe one fun activity that demonstrates the repulsion between like poles.
questions.answer: Placing magnets on matchbox cars so they push each other away without touching.
questions.solution: Fix magnets onto two toy cars with like poles facing each other. As one car is pushed toward the other, the second car will move away due to magnetic repulsion.
